reviews.review: If you enjoy PanzerGeneral -style games, this has some nice twists and mechanics to ye'olden ww2 combat units.

This is all I wanted in a 40K Game! Strategy in the vein of old-school games like Panzer, with all the options and units of 40k. Definitely loving this game, great choice if you're feeling some turn-based Warhammering!

If you want to play huge scale 40K battles, but don't have any money, then this is the game for you. An army that would normally cost thousands of pounds/dollars and hundreds of hours to amass, is created at the click of a button. All the Leman Russ Variants are in here. Same with most of the Imperial Super Heavy tanks. Also, yes, Titans. Warhounds and Reavers for days. 

Now, admittedly, the graphics and sound are very basic. Animations are non-existent. The turn limit is rather annoying and the difficulty varies from level to level. However, if you want to feel like you are commanding a huge Imperial force on the battlefield, this game is for you.
Classic game to play over and over again
Great Game!
Awesome 40k hex game, no complaints as I got it on deep sale.

Very true to the old Armegeddon game. Wish the graphics were a bit more updated. Game is difficult right from the start. You have to slowly progress or you can get overwhelmed quickly. Also, tutorial should do better about informing you about purchasing new units. However, gameplay is solid and enjoying it. Love that there are so many types of units. I should research what the stats mean though.
me like gaem
Like panzer general, but W40k
fun 40k stratagy game reminds me of reading some of the grand scale of the wars on armageddon
Solid 40k take on Panzer General game play style.  If you like Panzer General and 40K then this is a no brainer.  I also like the associated DLC.
pretty good turnbased strategy

Barely passing grade. The game works (crashes every once in a while though). The game turns go pretty fast. I really don't mind that there is very little animation, the actual unit sprites look good and the sound effects are not bad, some of them quite good actually. I'm satisfied with the graphics and the interface, and the game has a decent amount of content. If you create your own rules for yourself, you can make the game challenging and somewhat interesting, using a wide range of well known Imperial guard and space marine units. 

Even though this game is more polished, I would say that I still prefer the old Final Liberation, which was a lot more dynamic than this game. It had its own ballance issues, but I think it had a better morale/ victory point system, and there were more interesting differences between the strengths and weaknesses of different units. 

I don't know if I've ever played a strategy game with such one-dimensional gameplay before. It doesn't seem to encourage any kind of complex tactics, and the gameplay is pretty repetitive. 

There is a huge variety of units, but they're poorly ballanced, especially not on higher difficulty. 

The best tactic seems to be to rely very heavily on the toughest units.

The Furioso Dreadnought unit seems to be the best unit in the game, followed by the other space marine dreadnoughts, which are also excellent. At max difficulty, most units just seem to be virtually useless, as they cease to be effective even against the units which they should have a big advantage against. Armour and durability is absolutely essential for most of your fron-line units. Most infantry just sucks at the high difficulty settings, it dies way too quickly even when trying to fight lower quality infantry out in the open. 

That being said, I kind-of prefer using infantry, but it seems to make more sense to do it on a lower difficulty setting. You need to create your own restrictions so that the game isn't too easy on those lower difficulty settings though.

Only infantry can enter buildings and a lot of objectives, so you will usually need a couple of infantry units for that

If you like 40K and hex-based wargames...

...just buy it!

36,99$ for a game of 5$ quality and "depth". Boring, buggy, random, cheap and another kick in the face for Warhammer fans. Avoid Slitherine products at all costs. They are already a meme for their pricing-quality ratios.
like it thank you.
A good successor to the old SSI games
Best Warhammer game I played so far. A very good turn-based game.
Don't bother with PanzerCorps. This is the true successor to Panzer General II.

Bought this game, was expecting it be similar to Panzer Corps, though I have to say, it's substantially better in every regard. The editor is very powerful, yet simple to use. I was able to edit and modify maps and units within minutes. Speaking of units, they look fantastic, there are about 700 or so of them with the expansions. This game is engaging and more immersive than Panzer Corps (I own the iOS version and PC versions). I'm exceptionally pleased with what Slitherine has put forth, multiple and diverse factions to play with as well. Very nice job, fantastic game!
A nice move & shoot turn-based strategy game well done. If you like that kind of game, you'll like this one.

Fantastic Game! Can't speak for every DLC, but overall, this is a great game. I can only begin to theorize about why the critic reviews are lukewarm.

I think the game is beautiful, with amazing animations taking center stage for the visuals. They remind me of the explosions of world in conflict, that's how good they are. It is really fun to play the imperial campaign, and the story is certainly entertaining enough for a Warhammer 40k game, and the voice acting is quite a bit better than I would expect from an independent project like this one. The sound is a little less impressive, but it does a good job at setting the Warhammer 40k tone nonetheless. The tactics are good, and the progression through the campaign alongside your units is absolutely fun to say the least. Most of the time, missions are "more of the same," but that is a good thing since the tactics, combat, units and action are great. The game includes an amazing roster of units as well, which is sure to please any Warhammer 40k fan. I absolutely love how these units play and feel, and most of them are great fun to utilize.

That's not to say that there aren't any flaws. For one, the game is a bit unbalanced. There are certain units that are just so obviously better than others in terms of cost/benefit ratio that some of the units will, doubtlessly, just never be utilized, which is a real shame. Another thing I should mention is that mission objectives can be somewhat ambiguous on some missions, requiring a restart after an unexpected "defeat" screen. But this is rare, and thus not too bothersome. Another flaw often mentioned is the AI. I played the main campaign on "challenging" difficulty, and this is probably the one the game is balanced for. I think it's a lot of fun to play on this difficulty, but it is true that the AI sometimes makes quite questionable decisions which can be exploited to great effect. Then again, the enemies *are* orks, so what can one expect? It would be nice if I could change the difficulty after starting the campaign, but I have not seen the option. That is a problem too. Finally, there are some graphical bugs that can require a game restart, but really, I don't find that any of these things detract too much from the fun of gameplay during single player. Due to the imbalance, multiplayer probably wouldn't be quite the same amount of fun, however.

Overall, I think that this is a fantastic game. I will play the DLCs, and I will probably purchase the ork "expansion." I really hope that we will see more of the same content from the developers. I would love another game where we play Adeptus Sororitas vs Chaos or Dark Eldar, for example. Any combination of factions that aren't usually covered in these games would be amazing. I would gobble it up. But, for $40, the game is kind of a difficult sale, being such an indie title relying on such ancient game engines, so I understand why people might be so harsh on it. Still though, I had more fun playing this game than I have playing at least 60% of AAA titles. Honestly! This game is great, as long as you can look past the flaws and think of it less as a seriously competitive title, and more as a fun, challenging, and relaxing experience on a weekend when you have the time. If you are a warhammer 40k fan, and you can appreciate turn based titles, you owe it to yourself to get this game. It is amazing.

WH40K: Armageddon is a nostalgic throwback to 20+ years ago for me. It blends the feel of Games Workshop's tabletop Epic 40K variant with the mechanics of early hex-based PC strategy games like Battleground Ardennes (from... 1995?!?? I feel so old, sigh).

There's familiar fun to be had here for someone like me who has fond memories of the classics that inspired this game. It scratches a specific itch for me, but I can see why some folks would hate it and dismiss it as dated and clunky.

Below I'll add a list of good and bad stuff as I think of it.

+ If you are a fan of classic hex-based strategy and 40K, this game will appeal for sure.
+ Simple gameplay that is easy to pick up and play a mission or two.
+ Personally I love the basic, clean graphics.

= Mediocre voice acting and plot, but I can live with it.

- UI is massively dated, and not in a cute retro sense.
- No way to rebind keys in game. Surely any game released in the 21st century should have this function?
- Arbitrary turn caps. In some missions I finished 10 turns early, on one occasion I 'lost' after hitting 15/15 with every Ork unit dead and a unit 1 hex from the remaining victory point. Really annoying.
- A low unit cap means you can't choose quantity over quality, e.g. you cannot use dozens of guardsmen units as a meatshield. You are railroaded into fielding elite units, so it turns into an Astra Militarum-skinned panzer game.
- Loads of units to choose from, but only a few worth using unless you want to roleplay.
- Confusing naming choices for many units. Some will definitely stick out as confusing or plain wrong if you know 40K lore.
- Implementation of flyers seems incomplete, rendering them basically useless.

The game appeared overpriced to me for what it was, so it sat on my wishlist for a long time. I finally grabbed it with all the DLC for about £7.50 on Fanatical, and I'd say it's definitely worth it to me at that price.

An amazing strategy game similar to Panzer Corps, Panzer General or Iron Storm (from the Sega Saturn) set in the WH 40k universe. WIth all the DLC you're getting a ton of conent here and some nice story driven campaigns. The playable factions are Space Marines, Imperial Guard and Orks (with DLC). There are also mods for Tau and Tyranid if that is of interest to you. If you like either WH 40k or the hex strategy genre this is a must buy.

'Oomiez iz rubbish
We'ze gonna Krump de lot
Not Enuff dakka!!!
Lovely little wargame, much better than I expected, a must-play for any Warhammer 40K/Strategy fan !
Panzer General in the 40K universe!
Lots of fun, miniature war-game based play.

From the little I played got the impression it's overly simplistic with very little strategy. Missions are so short and limit you to so few turns that it's just a matter of moving your units and hoping they can either rush past the enemy to achieve the goals or outshoot the orks/chaos forces due to sheer luck. It looks like that classic Warhammer Liberation game from the late 90's but that one had real depth to it, this is nothing like that. It's more like a mobile game where you move units and hope for the best in super-fast, extra-short missions. The story and voice acting are excellent, so it's a shame. 4/10
Best Stratagy game I have played in a long time!

-Probably a bit too tedious for most people. This game is missing some quality of life features, like finding units that have and haven't moved or attacked. There is a way to cycle through units, but it just doesn't help much.

-They AI didn't seem very responsive. Sometimes they would just wait for many turns, not moving, not attacking, just waiting for me to trigger some script.

-The units stick around from battle to battle, and you can change a unit's role/job before each battle without penalty. Plus, the unit keeps its experience. Nice.

-The ability to pick your dialogue in conversations is a nice touch. It makes the game seems much more immersive. A really old feature that feels fresh.

-I only got into a few a of the story scenes, but I had a hard time caring about what story was. Was there a story? I don't remember.

-Personally, I would pick up Battle for Wesnoth instead. The game play feels about the same, but I liked it much better.

Warhammer Panzer General. As a member of Imperial Guard you have to stop Orkish invasion on your homeworld. Having in disposal a great variety of in the lore units, and looking at in the lore cliches.
For fans of Panzer General and/or Warhammer.
its a fun game thats tells a diffent story then most of the other warhammer games

Everything beats everything. Everything beats nothing. I really didn't understand what was happening in this game, which is really bad for a tbs. If you wanna scratch your Warhammer 40k itch and still be in the same genre, I recommend playing Warhammer 40k Sanctus Reach.

Very fun tactical wargame in the vein of Panzer Corps (in fact it's very similar). It gives you the sense of massive 40k battles quite well. There are a huge amount of units that you unlock throughout the campaign so that means you can go back and try different army compositions. The main campaign can get pretty hard, but is fair and ultimately beatable (unlike some of the DLCs). There are a few improvements that could be made like, air units being able to stack with ground units and the possibility of scouting units, but hopefully these could be incorporated into Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon 2. Overall recommended if you like turn-based wargames.

Love turn based strategy, love 40k, and this has many of the features that would make for an excellent game much like the earlier games in their vein from GW, and Axis and Allies, Panzer Corps etc.

Sadly the level design is awful, the script was written by someone who has never played 40k and/or failed creative writing, the units are too samey, the only strategy is to out-range your opponent, none of the development mechanics are ever explained, if they do anything at all, and every mission feels exactly the same: Find a bottleneck, hold it.

Its so sad to see two of my favourite things put together so badly.

The imperium, even the forces commanded by you, seem terribly disorganised, you never have any intelligence and know nothing of the forces you face, your auxillary troops start isolated and without any support, their only role every time is to die whilst you get your troops to a bottleneck. Why you couldnt just deploy them at the bottleneck is never actually explained... And most missions you go into with no clear objective... Then the AI will litterally sometimes sit out of range turn after turn and get blown up by your artillery. 
This last one I'm actually glad of, as it makes a change to the usual find a bottleneck and wait approach, but it is still clearly a failing.
Gawd, the list of awful just goes on an on.

The good:
- Looks. The units are detailed if not animated, the firing animations are varied and pretty. It isnt going to win any graphical awards, but someone has put a lot of time into the units and it shows. Maps are ok, but unexceptional.
- Voiced Story. They had the right idea, they link together the encounters and break them up. Sadly the script sucks and the voice acting is average, this last part I dont mind at all, I'll take average voice acting over a terrible script 100% of the time. I want to feel more fervor from the imperiums xenophobes, I want to fear the commisars, not give them backchat, and having a random comms officer tell them they are wrong is bananas...
- Unit progression. It...at least looks like... it has unit progression. And you keep your troops, this is excellent! Sadly I havent the faintest idea what gathering experience does, there are no abilities to unlock, stats dont appear to improve, other than they have a little bar of skulls that goes up and down (as casualties are taken).
- Enjoyable, untill you realise the "progress" is all an illusion. Units gain nothing, the "story" is a linked series of random encounters, with random units, deployed in random places for presumably random reasons.
fun game to play BUT when the save game function keeps crashing the game it is impossible to play!! this is something that NEEEDED to be fixed in a patch  before now. save your money

It was an OK game. The DLC wasn't really worth it because the base game wasn't enjoyable enough to warrant the purchase. 
I not really sure how I'd improve the game, so I can't really offer many suggestions but it was definitely missing something

Pro:
- Only W40k game in this "style" so far
- It's rather simplicistic but still a challenge to some point
- The variety of units is awesome

Con:
- You need some time to get used to the game
- A little bit of brown in brown and grey in grey

All in all I really enjoyed the game.

A very nice wargame with a cool vibe. In the first missions you are purposefully put in losing situations, making you feel even better when, later on, your army start to push back the enemy with your now experienced (and beloved) troops. It's hard, but extremely satisfying. I suggest to play at the almost hardest difficulty: it really helps to keep the player always on the edge of a terrible situations, so you have to consider very well if it is better to use your few resources to reinforce now your battered infantry team, or to wait and buy a new tank at the end of the mission, or to stockpile them for an even bigger tank later on.
fun game if you like the turn based wargames and it has a warhammer setting. bought on sale. no regrets.  not much replay value but it was a good go. i still have the DLCs to complete eventually.

15$ Game
45$ Price Tag

Looks horrible and plays pretty bad.
Campaign is pretty boring and just sorta not fun, mission objectives are rather 'obscure'
like the game reminds of an older title cant quite remember its name but game mechanics are very much the same.
Fun game but because of the round limit most of your creativity comes in the form of unit composition and placement.

I thought this was a good game to play.  It's only four years old, but it reminds me of the old Panzer General games from the late 1990s in both graphics, sounds, and features.  I got it on sale for a fair price, which is why I went through with the purchase.  However, after playing it for a while, I didn't really see the point in any of it... sort of like Panzer General.  I guess that if I had some care about the Warhammer universe, then maybe that would have added something to it for me, but I don't know.  Really, for the $17 I paid for the complete edition, I didn't think it was really worth it, which is why I refunded it.  To be honest, I would recommend this game in its complete version if it was being sold for $4.99.  Above that, and I am not sure you would get your money's worth.  But as far as the genre goes, it is nevertheless fun.

This game started out alright as a functioning turn-based strategy in the WH40k universe and something to play when limited to a laptop without much gaming power. It showed some promise in the gameplay style despite a general lack of production value and polish. The interface is not great and it was difficult to determine the controls for some functions.

The real showstopper for me though were missions that couldn't be completed, not because the play was difficult or even challenging, but because the poorly-indicated success conditions didn't work even when achieved. There are some really unfortunate, even amateurish, problems in the premise and execution of certain missions' design. I could probably forgive if it was just nonsensical objectives communicated poorly, but it's worse than that: sometimes the game just doesn't give you the win even when you've verified on the dev's own forums what the actual win conditions for a particular mission are.

When I first bought this I played ten hours and gave up on it because I couldn't progress in the campaign due to the above issues. I took a few months off, tried again from the beginning and gave it another honest shot. Same issues, despite the devs having clearly taken some steps to recognize the probems players are having: responses of instructions and walkthroughs on the game forums, videos and screenshots from beta testers. One ugly bit is that they patched some ingame "tips" in: essentially minor spoilers pushed on the player to mitigate the bad mission design. If you have to do that, the problems run deep. If it's unwinnable without knowing the twists ahead of time, and STILL unwinnable even then because the game simply does not function as intended, that is a bad situation.

I wish I had not supported this game and this developer. Avoid.
Such a awesome game, you won't be disappointed if you like hex based combat games. They did a good job with this one, especially if you like the Warhammer franchise.

A decent game.  I am playing it for the Mac OS.  There are occasional crashes.   The main campaign is fun.  The expansions are a little repetitive.  I have played through Vulkan's Wrath and I am half way through the Blood Angels' campaign.  Aside from minor improvements, like a slight gain in requisition points per turn,  I don't really notice any differences in the DLC so far.  Hopefully that will change in the other expansion.  As of now, I give the game a 7/10.
This is a great top down tactical war game for 40k.   I've got all the expansions and have played through most of them.

I haven't gotten very far into the game yet, but I can tell it's pretty good. The turn-based combat is quite involved, and I'm enjoying it. 

My only gripes, and the reason I wanted to write this review, is that some of the writing is abysmal, and while the extensive voice acting is of good quality, they mispronounce a ton of the lore-specific words.
 
I guess this is my way of saying that this game is very close to being pretty great. I just wish they'd hired better writers who understood the lore of the game well enough to avoid sounding fairly silly. 
Still, it's more than most games have.

The animations leave a bit to be desired, but it's generally a fairly decent-looking game.
Very plain gameplay, bad tutorial, miserable sounds, no music, no effects, no atmosphere...
I would recomend this to anyone who likes a strategy game.  The only shame is there isn't more content to buy.

This is an almost perfect Warhammer 40k game. Add in a mod or two (look it up online, you will be glad you did!) and the game gets even better. 

Also: it frequently goes on sale for a very reasonable price. 

I highly recommend it :-)

It's a Panzer Corp knock off, as Rites of War was a Panzer General knockoff.  It's a very different game though and not in a great way.  I like this game, but I really love both 40k and Panzer Corps.  It's nowhere near as complex as PC or as a good, but if you really love 40k and hex games it's 5-10$ not too badly spent.  I wouldn't get it at full price.  The DLC is a bit of a rip off too.  I really hope that when Panzer Corp II drops we'll get a better iteration.  I can get somewhat frustrating to play this game given that the difficultly levels are not set very well, so watch out of that.  On the hardest levels it's pretty close to unbeatable.  You should also savescum liberally.

I thought it would be a fun turn-based strategy, but man was I dead wrong. Horrible UI, mechanics that just don't make sense (It tries to copy the tabletop in a hilariously bad manner), and it crashed within 15 mins of play. Another 40k game loaded into the garbage pile. 

I'm SO glad I didn't buy this at full price, because it's not even worth $10. Thank god for refunds!

**RECOMMEND - GETTING ON 75% OFF SALE**

It gives a nice relaxing and scale-accurate look at battles in the 41st Millenium. 
Want to send Legions of Imperial Soldiers to die in battle against tides of Orks? You can.

Want to have a Vanguard of Sentinels scout ahead with elite infantry and armor sweeping in behind for the killing blows with Artillery support? You can.

If you like turn-based games quite a bit like the old Warhammer 40K Tabletop games, this is your game. Overall I like it and it is a relief that this is decent considering the garbage over-hyped Warhammer 40K games have been such as DOW 3, Space Marine, the RTS, etc.

The only negative I can find is that the story in the campaign is very hollow and doesn't really immerse you into the Warhammer 40K universe very much and the game play gets old after a bit.

Here are my impressions of the game and its DLC campaigns, I wrote them each as I progressed through the game, I picked the middle 'challenging' difficulty and I think that was the right choice to have a good balance of difficulty and fun, not difficult enough to require constant reloading of saves to get perfect attack rolls all the time, but still hard enough to only win by a hair on some missions.

some overall positives and negatives:

[b]+[/b] hex-based turn based strategy in the WH40k Universe
[b]+[/b] the story is typical, but fitting
[b]+[/b] no bugs/crashes encountered
[b]+[/b] not too easy and given the 5 possible diffiulties offered everyone should find their place
[b]+[/b] good combat and atmosphere sounds
[b]+[/b] 100 hours of playtime if you do all the DLCs
[b]+[/b] the outcome prediction before attacking really helps making the right decisions and learning

[b]-[/b] no cinematics (if you paid full price this might be a let down)
[b]-[/b] the units have only limited animations(ie when attacking expect some scale fitting fireworks)
[b]-[/b] the difficulty curve is a bit off sometimes from mission to mission
[b]-[/b] some of the mechanics have to be found out the hard way
[b]-[/b] the mission objective descriptions are a bit lacking, like for extra objectives or changing goals
[b]-[/b] there is a big selection of units,but some units are just fluff / minor weapons changes

[u][b]Base Campaign[/b][/u]

This takes you through the whole story with text dialogues inbetween missions, sometimes giving you choices and fitting voiceovers. The campaign is divided into multiple acts and several missions each, its easy to get 30-40 hours out of it. You play with Imperial Guard units in the beginning and around the middle of the campaign you get some access to Space Marine Units and finally Titans for the last third or so. On the challenging difficulty mode it was indeed sometimes challenging, but if you planned solid from the beginning of the mission a total restart due to turn limit constraints was only two times necessary for me. Nothing stops you from using savegames whenever you want to scout the map or try different deployment options and positions. The campaign is actually really well done in the sense of turning the tide feeling, going from desperation to relieve and the last missions you can field enough powerful units to rightfully avenge the fallen.

[u][b]Vulkan's Wrath - Salamander Campaign[/b][/u]

This is the perspective of the Salamanders, so story line wise you start around in the middle, drive the enemy back, liberate and sometimes hold the line against desperate enemy attempts to overrun you. While you get some story dialogues like in the base campaign, they have no voiceover which is a bit jarring. The missions are in a way harder than the base game due to the limited selection of units, you have to find a new way to get things done, especially if you are used to full artillery + titans, however the Salamanders have above average amor thus their tanks and Dreadnaughts are sturdy enough to get things done, and their selection of infantry can have a big impact too, however be prepared to take some losses. The maps for the mission are sometimes very large and require you to cover some distance or manage your own and alot of additional units, but its all managable and may the emperor be with you. Many of the missions have side objectives that reward you with extra units if you manage to complete them, however due to turn limits and massive amounts of enemies it might require to find a whole new strategy to complete those on top of the original mission.

[u][b]Angel's Death - Blood Angels Campaign[/b][/u]

Not really a favorite of mine, no voiceovers for the inbetween mission speeches, no side objectives encountered, at least you can pick the battlefield of your choice for the last mission (another hive indoor, or an easy defense). While the inside hive missions force you to use alot of infantry units, the amount of strategy you can use to get things done faster is rather limited, especially with the enemy reinforcing his units at every chance you give him (3 units adjacent right next to an enemy will completely stop that, but good luck with that, better kill outright) Just as with the Salamanders, Dreadnaught walkers made the backbone of my army again.

[u][b]Glory of Macragge - Ultramarines Campaign[/b][/u]

About as enjoyable as the Salamander campaign, a good mix of mission types and an epic battle (I picked the open field one) at the end. The sidemissions are quite hard if you play on a high difficulty and it would be nice to know if a mission as a side mission from the start, instead of stumbling on it or not. With the sometimes vast stretches between objectives you have to split up alot, disbanding units that have achieved their goals in order to have enough ressources to repair/fill up others that havent yet was not a rare occurence). Anyway a good conclusion to the Spacemarines action on Armageddon.

[u][b]Ork Hunters Campaign[/b][/u]

So far the easiest campaign, a bit shorter than the others too it seems, no optional mission goals, but you get to decide which end mission you want to do. The Ork Hunter units are quite OP (like each infantry unit is a team of soldiers as seen in the orignal predator movie) vs the feral ork units (and others too actually) but after some grueling missions in the space marines campaigns it was sort of a relief to have a relaxing orc stomping here and a bit of a continuation of the base campaign story.

[u][b]Golgotha Campaign[/b][/u]

A real nail-biting to the last man exhausting but fulfilling campaign. Next time an Exterminatus should be the option of choice for the planet. Anyway expect to have little ressources, weak units and be forced to play every trick (and savegame) up your sleeve to beat these missions, the number of easy ones I can count on 1 finger. Its actually my favorite of all DLCs, but I highly recommend playing all the others before as a preparation.
If you maybe like finnal liberation, then youll maybe like this game.
I would recommend this to anyone who likes Wh40k and strategy games. For me it's the first time I've played a hex-based strategy game and I am very happy
excellent enjoy this even more than panzer corp
Game is damn great. Even though it lacks some graphics and lacks feature of autosaving your progress, it is realy awesome

Game play & graphic in this game are great if you like playing turn baised games from the early 90's on the computer other than that this game makes Diablo 2 graphic look ground beaking good.

No worth wasting your money on

The "story" isn't something you play for, it's an ok turn based strategy set in Warhammer 40k featuring Space Marine chapaters against Orks, graphics wise it's simply put...bad. I think if this is the kind of game you're after check out Sanctus Reach instead, a much more polished 40k turn based game based entirely on the tabletop game. Overpricing is what I think has turned many people off.
cool stuff

This game is quite similar to Panzer Corps, which is a previous game made by the same studio. It is a significant improvement over the previous game in terms of having a lot more intressting units. If you liked Panzer Corps, you are probably going to like this one. If you tried and did not like Panzer Corps, this game is probably not for you.

If you have not played Panzer Corps, you might need an explanation of how this game works. It is a turn based tactics game with the main meat of the game being a campain. During the campain, you start of every mission with deploying units based on a point system, there are a LOT of units, and every single unit costs points, varying from 40 (a millita with pistols) to 2100 (a single giant mech). You can basically spend your points however you like until they run out or you run out of deployment slots, which are mainly there for stopping people from going all in on the millita with pistols. (which I would not recommend anyway)

Because of this system, this game is not about having the best units, it is about finding the units that are the most cost effective. Some units have specific traits, like commanders that help your units not get tierd and wanna go home, and support units that help providing counterattack vs anything that tries to hit stuff close to them. Building an army is a real puzzle, with a lot of valid options. Also, you get to keep your army from your previous mission, but can scrap and change units at will. This means that if you have found something that works, you don't have to re-build it for every mission which is nice. However, if you wanna do a full rebuild, you can just scrap everything and get your points back.

During your typical missions, you have to caputre and hold specific hexes. All missions are restricted to a specific number of turns, which means that there is some pressure for getting things moving vs taking things slow and not take as many losses. (Also, getting transports for your footsoldiers and stuff like that). Building up a working army and using it is a nice puzzle and I have had a lot of fun playing the main campain.

The few complaints I have are:

I would say it is a bit expensive for what it is, there is almost no animation, and only the main campain have voice acting (the DLC only have text). The graphics are functionall but won't set the world on fire. If you are unsure about if you like this kind of game, Panzer corps is about half the price and quite similar.

The difficulty settings are a bit rigid. There are 5 difficulty settings, and the jumps from one to another is rather large. It would be nice to have a few more. The difficulty can also not be changed after starting the campain, which made the end of some of the campains easier then some of the earlier missions as I understod the game way better at that point.

It would be nice to have some way of sorting your units better. There are filters, but even filtering only on say, tanks for the steel legion, you get like ~30 results. For example trying to find tanks that are good vs other tanks is kind of annoying, as the options you have are spread over a lot of different categories. This is not a major issue, but towards then end of the campain it gets a little silly.

Warrning for units with movement left when clicking "end turn" is not enabled by default... It is there in the setting, turn it on, will help a lot.

THE DLC:

So, I got the imperium eddition on sale, so I have tried all the DLCs. Basically, each of them introduces a new campain with a different imperial faction. It is nice to have something different to play if you get stuck on a mission, so I would recommend getting the bundle with all the DLC. The DLC campains are not so much about adding new units as they are about removing the standard tools forcing you to play differently. 

The spacemarine chapters have no cannon fodder soldiers or long range artillery, which forces some really different army designs. Those 3 DLC are quite similar, as space marine chapters are quite similar. Finnished the one for Salamanders, played a couple of maps of the other two.

Ork hunters are like steel legion but know what they are doing and bring knives to every gunfight... In that campain you are also fighting a lot of very poorly organized orks, but considering you legions motto is "bring a knife to a gunfight" that might be required to even the ods.

Haven't gotten far into the last campain yet.

CONCLUSION AND PLAYTIME:

So, in about 80 hours, I have finnished a playing the main campain once, and the salamander campain. As well as playing a couple of maps in all the other campains. I think Ill try the main campain on a harder difficulty again, as now I know all the units. The game features a "tutorial campain" which I highly recommend, it teaches you all you need to know. I haven't touched the easiest difficult setting, so I don't think this game would be that hard to enjoy even if you are not good at these sorts of games. 

There is multiplayer, which I haven't touched but there seemed to be games open. Might be fun vs a friend. Would expect this game to be about 160 hours of fun with all the DLC, a lot less without.

Panzer General/Corps with Orks, Titans and Space Marines!

Really, that's pretty much it.  If you don't know what Panzer Corps is, you should go and play it; it's an absolute classic wargame at this point.

If you don't know what Warhammer 40,000 is, I don't even know where to start explaining it.

Okay, there are some specific pros and cons to this title.  Firstly, the unit balancing is pretty terrible.  Most of the Steel Legion infantry options are unusably awful along with various other units on both sides which are either far too weak for their cost or just much too limited to be of use on any map with terrain features getting in your way.  The same with all the fliers for both sides.  They can do a bit of damage, yes, but they're going to be massacred as they do that bit of damage and are not useful as scouts.  Also, most Ork units are pretty weak for their cost, which only really matters in multiplayer, but is important if the multiplayer option is of interest to you.

Other than that, it's all good.  The graphics and sound are pretty much par for the Panzer Corps-course.  The mission design is pretty good and thankfully they leave the inevitable "hold out against overwhelming forces for X turns" until later in the campaign, not at the very beginning.  By that stage, you're pretty attached to those units and their precious experience, so watching them crumble and burn is especially painful, but in a nice piece of plot/design, the very next mission's just the point when you would likely be voluntarily disbanding a whole lot of these units in order to access some pretty phenomenal new shinies, so it really works out well.

All in all, a good choice, though if you dislike either the WH40K universe or Panzer Corps, this isn't likely to sway you.

Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon is a turn based stratgy game about using tanks to blow up orks! The voice acting is good, the art is nice, and the gameplay is smooth. You have at your fingertips many different units to command(of varying effectiveness).  8/10
So... that was it?

Surprisingly good game! A quite worthy Panzer General successor in the grim darkness of the far future, above and beyond my expectations. There was even one map which utilized maglec rail lines where your army could jump back and forth along the rail network while Orks attacked from different quarters. The campaign story was also surprisingly good, and true to the detailed background. The mysterious and seemingly sinister cult hinted at by the communications adjunct (who by the way helped drive home the massive casualties of the war) added an intriguing sideplot, and the game ending in the way it did was just right! The journey goes on.

Hats off to the developers! Well recommended.

This game isn't bad, but it's far from good.  It's a mediocre game all the way and medocrity spoils any long-term fun.

The idea of the game is fun enough and the first few missions are great, but ultimately the fun you have wears out as you slowly realize how shallow and poorly balanced everything is.  There are TONS of units available in the game - a good representation of the table-top offerings, but they're abysmally balanced and you won't use 90% of them.

There's very little agency in the units you field either.  Ostensibly, your units carry over after each mission to be used in later missions.  Realistically, however, even your most veteran squads will be mere cannon fodder as better units become available (Space Marines etc).  

I was ultimately disappointed in the purchase.  For $15-20, I'd have had no problem, but for +$35 there's just so little under the hood.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes Wh40k and strategy games. For me it's the first time I've played a hex-based strategy game and I am very happy with my purchase.

- There's tons of scenarios. I'm six hours in and I haven't even finished Act 1. Even without the DLC you'll have plenty of content.
- The huge unit roster gives you a lot of options to customize your army. Also adds replay value. 
- Many difficulty settings, for newbs and veterans alike. I'm playing on Normal and it feels like a challenge. 
- Feels like a Wh40k game. Atmosphere is good and ofcourse you're playing in a very famous war. 

I rarely write reviews, but I felt like giving something back to the devs for this great game.
Fun to play, moddable, captures the feel of WH40K universe pretty well. Love the range of units, all the way to Titans. Could use a scenario generator to improve replayability.

9 hours in and I am just in love with this game. I am reading books like Rise and Fall of the Third Reich and many books on strategy and this game sure satisify my war itch more than Panzer Corps. Sure Panzer Corps suppose to be realistic but this game is not too hard but still challenging and has scenerios that reflect how battles are really won. The graphics are better than many other type of games from this genre but not what Warhammer fans are use to. I love the story line of another ork  invasion too. The units are interesting and fun and I love using the terrian to its advantage. The game is lot more user friendly than other tactical turn based games. I cant believe im so deep in this game and yet only on the first chapter. Well worth the price to play.

first off if your looking for a hardcore 1:1 recreation of the tabletop this game isn't for you. If your a fan of old school turn based strategy useing a hex system of movement on the same level as Panzer Corps then this game is great! 

Along with some of the mods for this game mainly the "Tens of New Units" gives you a great deal of units to choose from.

Nice game. Sort of Panzer Corps but not boring! Zillions of units to choose from, in fact probably the best variety of Space Marine, Imperial Guard and Space Ork units available. I found myself spending a lot of time going through all the land and air units because this will give you the edge in combat - though there are some good tutorials to teach you the basics. Several campaigns based on Armageddon lore, loads of scenarios etc. and user made missions too. Nothing too complicated, just good old fashioned turn-based strategy, but sexed up considerably for the Warhammer 40k universe. I hope they keep adding dlc to the game.

Had this game before and returned it ,but I always wanted it and felt I never gave it any proper playtime ,finally gave in and play like crazy ,I love the low graphic ,large unit ,simple game style ,been playing for days ,I bought all the DLC's and have thouroughly enjoyed it

Warhammer 40K: Armaggeddon is a hex based game and is worth all the money that you'll be dishing out. The story is about the 1st invasion of Hive World of Armaggeddon. Which in case you don't know is a planet of huge mega city towers with everything wrong with china on steroids. In this polluted, firey wasteland; you are forced to repel and protect the planet from gajillions of Orks and DA BIGGEST BADDEST WARLORD AROUND Margaret Thatcher. 

The story is rather competent and is brought to life by the amazing cast of voice actors and art. The main story is divided into three acts, each of them all very meaty. If you manage to snatch this game on sale, I'll guarantee that you won't be testing out that refund button anytime soon.

I always found hex-based strategy games to be rather tedious due to how much number crunching you had to do but I have to say that the UI of Armageddon is the strongest part of the game. You can check what each stats do by clicking the stats and with the ability to compare units, I have to say I actually found out that I kinda knew what I was doing (A rare occurence I must admit).

There are so many different units in the game, all so full of flavor and tasty fluff. Commanders waving power swords, walking gun fortresses, and midget snipers. Combined with decent animations and sound effects I felt like I had a proper commander of the Steel Legion.

Now to summarise, despite my glowing review I did get overwhelmed by the sheer variety of options but take it slowly and check out different options and button. Experimentation is key. You'll notice that as the ball starts to get rolling, that it'll get harder to stop.

A awesome take on 40k! the campaign is challenging and the multiplayer is exciting.

Pros:
- SO. MANY. UNITS.
- great level detail
- a level editor
-many more good things 
Cons:
NADA

I actually love the artstyle and graphics of this game. I mean its not hard to look at the fucking screenshots to see if you are going to like it or not!. 

This is one of the best warhammer games i've played and one of the best Hex-based combat games too.  I can see $40USD for this might be an issue for some people but i got the game and all the DLC for $15 so im pretty pleased with it.

This game is definitely easy to learn, difficult to master. The basics behind the gameplay are deceptively simple, but as you unlock more and more units as you advance through the campaign, there are more options available and more potential "builds" for your army.

The campaign itself is both forgiving and rewarding.  With each new scenario, your units who survived the previous scenario will still be around, restored to full strength and retaining the combat experience they earned in prior battles.  However, at the same time you are given plenty of points to buy new units--even a whole new army if, say, you want to go from an infantry-heavy build to a few tanks and a titan. So it doesn't matter if all but a handful of your troops got wiped out the last mission (it's to be expected, what with the Imperial Guard and all), you can buy new ones next mission, or experiment with that new piece of artillery you just unlocked.

Terrible production values.  I'm used to strategy games having poorer graphics but this was really bad.  It is as if they just stuck a Warhammer sticker onto some no-name strategy engine and kicked it out the door.

Absolute CRAP. If you like Warhammer 40K, even to the slightest degree then DO NOT waste your money on this festering pustule of a game. it is NOTHING like 40K. The basic mechanics and gameplay is OK. Could use a bunch of small improvements like seeing a highlighted range/movement area before you move and shoot but it's not game breaking. What IS friggin game breaking is the B.S. unbelievable crap that infantry can do. Countless times I had a decent tank utterly destroyed by a pack of orcs with shotguns and cleavers. No, not heavy weapons or rocket launchers but goddam pea shooters and kitchen tools. This is just like the ludicrous stuff you see in the CIV games where some roman spearman can take out an Apache attack helicopter because...reasons. Also stuff appears out of goddam NOWHERE. You have extremely limited line of sightand many times your movement will be interupted as you come crashing into an enemy vehicle that you couldn't see. The game doesn't stop you 2 hexes short and ask if you want to continue, no, you just smack into it and auto attack if it's in your path, causing many expensive losses. It's great that you can earn experience but then again it doesn't seem to improve the units at all and they will all  die horribly before you get too good anyway. Even if you have a flying vehicle you can't see past 2-4 hexes. Sure these guys have interstellar travel but they have no concept of surveillance or even bloody binoculars. Oh and don;t expect Space marines or cool stuff like a Land Raider cos you're imperial guard in the Campaign so basically theres nothign cool. This is a ONE star game, end of story.

Well.. I never thought this would happen, but this title sucked me right into hex based wargaming.

Honestly I'm extremely narrow minded with my strategy games. Most of the time you can lose my by saying "Turn Based"... let alone "hex based", but after watching a few let's plays this one lodged in my mind and wouldn't desist until I tried it myself.

It really does look like arse... like you're fiddling with a playset made of kitchen magnets, sliding them across an out of scale representation of this industry and war wracked planet... buuuut spend a little time with it and you gradually find yourself mentally filling in the blanks... noting the wee access ramps in the trenchworks; correctly ascertaining the changes in elevation. Yeah, it's a computer game that makes you use your imagination. 

And really there's no excuse for that... at full price it's forty bucks for a title without a single animation (other than some weapon effects)... but... it's good.

It's actually really good. Ineffably good in fact. Watch some videos. It will either grab you or it won't... if it does it might very well convert you to a turny, hexy gamer (if you weren't already).

annnnnnnd it's better than DOW3

[h1]Turn-based tactics lite - with 40k flavor.[/h1]

I've been playing 40k video games since the days of SSi's Chaos Gate and Rites of War. Overall, this is an above average fare of turn based tactics and strategy. The strategic elements are light, and the difficulty isn't too high - any veteran of turn based hex strategy games will be able to breeze though.

[b]Breakdown:[/b]

[u]Story:[/u] 6/10 - For those familiar with the 40k universe, this is the 2nd war for armageddon. This game plays it safe and does nothing with the story other than tread through some basic plot points. Nothing to write home about, but the 40k world is rich in details and atmosphere, so it gets a slightly above average score for me. 

[u]Aesthetics:[/u]  8/10 - Solid showing of the 40k atmosphere and tone. There is some basic voice acting and still shots between missions with 40k art that feels like a part of the universe. The maps are pleasing to look at for the most part and the unit spirtes look about right. With that said, there was nothing that stuck out to me as being particularly exceptional. 

[u]Gameplay:[/u]  6/10 - This was a rather bog-standard hex based strategy game. The AI seemed rather overly-cautious and at no point did I feel like they were difficult. It is definitely fun moving units around and using superior positioning to destroy your enemies - but there's one major element that works against the game: a turn limit. This force a particularly aggressive mindset of play, and so the game will HEAVYILY favor certain units over others simply due to movemen rates. This feels like weak mechanics to me, as you will find yourself using the same tactics over and over to defeat your opponents. By mission 25, I had basically run an optimial set of units and tactics while the AI struggled to make a dent in my forces. 

[u]Stability:[/u]  8/10 - I had a very small handful of crashes during my playthrough. The game can be set up to save before every turn starts however, so no progress is really lost.

[u]Value:[/u]  4/10 - If I had purchased the vanilla game at full price ($39.99 USD) I would actually feel a bit disappointed. There are other DLC to purchase that will increase playtime - but I haven't tried it yet. Considering that you have to pay for it, that doesn't change my mind one bit regarding the value. I would think that $25 - $30 is a far more fair price for the game that you get.

tl;dr: If you liked SSI's Rites of War, it is basically that game but better. Also, if you like reliving the 2nd War for Armageddon, it does do that. With that said, I'd be hesitent to buy at full price, but it is definitely worth it around the 25-30 dollar mark.

Quick! What's a medusa seige gun and what's the difference between the medusa and the armageddon pattern medusa?

If you can answer this question, congrats, you understand the main draw of this game. It has a stupidly in depth unit roster that reads more like forgeworld's catalogue. It has detail in wierd places, from Von Strabb's speaches, to even naming the rivers you're fighting over. It's pretty fun if you like 40k and know armageddon because you can understand how many grots you just blew up.

Other than that, the timer really REALLY kills the game. It sets the pace for the almost every mission. So many missions had to be repeated because you had it in the bag but couldn't move a unit fast enough for your last turn. I really wish that the AI was just cranked up to be a bit less static and it's not a race to go from 1 strongpoint to the other within x amount of turns.

Also the price, 40 bucks is a steep price to pay for a game with some issues in UI department and the fact that it does have work put into it, but it looks really really cheap. Good sale title if you like turn based strategy and also like to get all grognardy about 40k. But seriously a community mod to take away that timer in exchange for a more genuine difficulty might be greatly appreciated... get on it lads.
Good game, but haven't played for awhile.  Plan to come back.  High end game for cheap when it's on sale.

Playing different turnbased games (CIV V, Stargeneral, Steelpanthers: World at war, 1914, Battle Isle 2 u.a.) WH 40.000: Armageddon is disappointing. Why?
- High price (36 €). I have bought for 18 € on a selling offer day. 18 €? Right. Also expensiv. Maybe ist just 8 € worth?! I do not know.
- I played Wh 40k tabletop years ago. But the feeling from the past cannot be found at this game
- Low graphics seem to be from the early 2000s.
- Variety of units is unnecessary because comparing units leads straight to an army compilation consisting of specific units you don not change until you find stronger ones. There was no reasing using a different types of units.
- Aircraft are expensiv to buy and to maintain and eventually very vulnerable.
- Artillery has high range but low efficiency. Thats strange.
- Experience levels exists. But i can feel no advantage. More damage? No. Maybe a little bit more but it is not decessive.

I love this game, I'm currently on my 3rd play through of the main campaign and can honestly say that this game deserves praise. the story is well told with good voice acting and even a couple of dialogue options in it. while the graphics aren't high end to me that adds charm as well as ease of use due to the low requirements of the game. If there was a complaint I would have about this game it's that it doesn't stagger out unit advances fast enough causing you to get access to a better unit AND that unit's upgrade at the same time which sadly makes the Macharius heavy tanks redundant since a Baneblade just works better. I will admit that 40$ is a bit rich for this game but I lucked out and got the game with all DLC for 23$, an awesome deal since those are all great as well (well maybe except for one). if you're a fan of 40K or just getting into it like me, or maybe you just want a little sci-fi in your wargames then I would definitely suggest you pick this one up.... on sale of course this is Steam.
Purge the alien, the mutant, the heretic. The Emporer protects.

It's Review time!

another review from me where this time, I review Warhammer 40k game, it's about 2nd-3rd Armageddon Wars where both wars are between Imperium and Orks and what I like most from this game is: More Realistic Personnel damage system than even 40k Tabletop game(in Tabletop, Astartes has no more wounds than Average Person(other than they're tougher and wears Power Armor) while here, Space Marines have more Hit Points than average Guardsmen) and this game plays lot like Final Liberation
Captures the essense of the Second War for Armageddon. Commissar Yarrick would endorse your participation in this war.

While it does have a hefty pricetag and weak graphics, Warhammer 40k: Armageddon still delivers copious amounts of deep strategy, customization, and sheer fun. The game, while it can seem a bit obtuse at first, has a certain degree of raw charm that helps committed players overcome the pricetag, graphics, and lack of overall animations. This is, afterall, a computer version of a tabletop wargame and, thus, the gameplay itself must truly shine for it to be worth playing while bereft of many of the bells & whistles gamers have grown accustomed to. I am of the belief that it does! I love this game and think it's worth the money, especially if you can manage to get the Feature Game + DLC bundle on sale. Hope this helps!

It is just simply fun to me. I enjoyed it a lot and just find it is so hard to stop playing. 

By the way, I am a strategy game fan and I like the theme of Warhammer 40k. The feature I like most is the oppotunity of mixing Guardsmen and different Space Marine Chapters. It's THE only game I've played that allows players to do that.

Hope they can make another game with more races. It'll be even cooler!

As an avid games workshop fan from back in the 80's, I have experinced Gazkhul's emergence from a pre battle feild write up, to pinnacle ork character table top personality, and into fabled legend through the armeggeddon summer campaign way back in 3rd ed where I did the majority of my own gaming.

   This video game reconnects me to the nostalgia of those moments and leaves me with a kind of glee to approach each new tactical puzzle to see how this game models diffrent aspects of long familiar combat dynamics at a pseudo Epic scale. Although the rule set is blatantly diffrent and the voice acting on the verge of corny, I feel like the enviornment is wonderfully '40k feeling' immersive. On top of that the game that it is is VERY good. I started playing on hard difficulty right away and quickly foun d myself having to think about how to use my forces diffrently to acheive success of objectives while completely minimizing losses where I could.

   The sheer ammount of content translates to being able to play any particular favorites (although sadly not my beloved Space Wolves) and having as much as I want of poking around with as much variance in approach I want to take. the sheer AMMOUNT of diffrent units availible ensures I will have as much as my appetite craves. Wolfsinn's summary; best video war game I have bought in a decade.

Not a bad game, but kind of expensive for what it is, if you're a 40K fan and it's on sale it's worth a go.

The DLCs are something to avoid, they are basically extra maps with a more limited troop selection, you'd need to be really into the game to enjoy them.

Great game. It is NOT Panzer corps in 40k setting, as they changed the mechanics:
- No free replacements (they always cost XP, even the automatic end-of-mission ones): I had an elite infantry unit defend a choke point, killing multiple units, began the mission ~350 exp, finished with >500. But as it took losses, after replacements it was down to 380. So all that experience was in fact lost.


Compared to Final Liberation, I really hate thgat all of a unit's weapons are fired at once, at the same target. I guess that's because it's at a "higher" strategic level, not like Final liberation where 1 unit was exactly 1 unit. One downside of this is that artillery units which can fire over obstacles, can also do so with their heavy bolters, which should not happen.

I really enjoy this game, regardless of its simple graphics and sounds.
It makes more fun to play than most of the 'modern' titles,
and the story is quite nice (although the way it's told is like the rest of the graphics no longer up to date).

But what really matters is that the game is a real game,
no movie to play or sth similar, it's just a very good game, with some suprises
no more, no less

In short: I recommend this game to all fans of strategic games who are not in need of up to date graphics. If you try it, you will really be rewarded
